turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has our opinion this morning in case 14-1055, Lightfoot versus Cendant Mortgage Corporation.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: This case began when petitioners, a mother and daughter, filed suit after the refinancing foreclosure and sale of their family home. The respondents here, Cendant Mortgage Corporation and Fannie Mae, were named as defendants. Petitioners filed their suit in state court and Fannie Mae removed the case to federal district court. The district court denied a motion to send the case back to state court and the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit affirmed. We now reverse. Fannie Mae is a government sponsored, but privately owned corporation. By statute, it has the power to sue and to be sued and to complain and to defend in any court of competent jurisdiction, state or federal. In removing the case to federal district court, Fannie Mae relied on this provision, known as a sue-and-be-sued clause as the basis for federal jurisdiction over petitioner's suit. This Court has held that some sue-and-be-sued clauses do confer jurisdiction on the federal courts. Under our cases, a sue-and-be-sued clause will not be read to confer jurisdiction on the federal courts unless it specifically mentions the federal courts. Fannie Mae's sue-and-be-sued clause does reference the federal courts. But it limits that reference to federal courts of competent jurisdiction. This Court has not, until this case, addressed a-sue-and-be-sued clause that limits its reference to the federal courts in this way. This Court has, however, already explained that a court of competent jurisdiction is a court with a grant of subject-matter jurisdiction that gives it the power to hear the case before it. Fannie Mae's sue-and-be-sued clause thus expressly refers to suits in federal courts that already possess subject-matter jurisdiction over a case involving Fannie Mae. That express reference means that the clause does not itself grant federal courts jurisdiction over those cases. Instead, it permits suit in a federal court already endowed with subject-matter jurisdiction. Fannie Mae has raised several arguments based on the statutory text and on its understanding of Congressional purpose, but none are strong enough to overcome the clear text of Fannie Mae's sue-and-be-sued clause. The judgment of the Ninth Circuit is reversed. The decision of the Court is unanimous.
